introSection: The Stratosphere Tower Fishhook Coaster is a cancelled shuttle roller coaster by Arrow Dynamics that was to be installed at Stratosphere Tower in the early 2000s. It was to be a tall shuttle coaster that was shaped like a fishhook. The coaster was cancelled due to the manufacturer's bankruptcy.

sections.title: History
sections.content:
In 2001, Arrow Dynamics planned a tall fishhook coaster to be built at Stratosphere Tower in Las Vegas, Nevada. It was going to be tall and reach a top speed of. The car would ascend an elevator lift and would be held over. The car would drop and go over Las Vegas Boulevard. It would then hit a tall vertical rollback above Paradise Road.

The vertical rollback was close to a suburban community and a neighborhood, and residents complained that the coaster would be too loud. They didn't want to be distracted by the noise the coaster would make. Plus, the screams coming from passengers would also annoy the residents.

In February 2002, Las Vegas officials decided to reduce the height to and the speed to. The vertical rollback would also be reduced to. Also, the ride vehicles would be enclosed to reduce noise. The city council would begin construction in April. However, Arrow Dynamics filed for bankruptcy on December 3, 2001, and officially closed on October 28, 2002.

fullText:
The Stratosphere Tower Fishhook Coaster is a cancelled shuttle roller coaster by Arrow Dynamics that was to be installed at Stratosphere Tower in the early 2000s. It was to be a tall shuttle coaster that was shaped like a fishhook. The coaster was cancelled due to the manufacturer's bankruptcy.

In 2001, Arrow Dynamics planned a tall fishhook coaster to be built at Stratosphere Tower in Las Vegas, Nevada. It was going to be tall and reach a top speed of. The car would ascend an elevator lift and would be held over. The car would drop and go over Las Vegas Boulevard. It would then hit a tall vertical rollback above Paradise Road.

The vertical rollback was close to a suburban community and a neighborhood, and residents complained that the coaster would be too loud. They didn't want to be distracted by the noise the coaster would make. Plus, the screams coming from passengers would also annoy the residents.

In February 2002, Las Vegas officials decided to reduce the height to and the speed to. The vertical rollback would also be reduced to. Also, the ride vehicles would be enclosed to reduce noise. The city council would begin construction in April. However, Arrow Dynamics filed for bankruptcy on December 3, 2001, and officially closed on October 28, 2002.
